worth_it_explanation: Pacing and under-delivery tracking decide whether a guaranteed campaign reaches its committed impression count smoothly across the flight or stumbles into a make-good. The check reviews how line items are paced and whether under-delivery risk surfaces early enough to act on, so it costs reading time in the SpringServe console rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete delivery setting or a monitoring gap the publisher can close before a guarantee is missed.
intro: A guaranteed or direct-sold campaign in SpringServe carries a committed impression count that the line item must deliver across its flight. Pacing controls how that volume is spread over time, and the difference between a line item that paces smoothly toward its goal and one that front-loads or stalls is the difference between a campaign that lands on its number and one that ends in a make-good or a missed guarantee. This subcategory covers two related questions. The first is whether line items are paced in a way that fits the goal rather than burning the budget early or leaving it for the end. The second is whether under-delivery risk is monitored on a cadence that catches a shortfall while there is still flight left to recover it.

audit_checks.title: Pacing mode does not fit the line item's delivery goal
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A pacing mode that does not match the goal changes when impressions are delivered without changing the total. Front-loaded or ASAP pacing on a line item meant to spread evenly burns volume early and risks a dry second half, while even pacing on a campaign that needs an early burst can leave it short. The result is uneven delivery against a fixed guarantee, which a smooth pace would have avoided.

educate.base:
SpringServe paces a line item's budget across its flight using a pacing mode, and the mode decides the shape of delivery over time rather than the total volume. The total is fixed by the goal. The mode decides whether that volume arrives evenly day by day, in an early burst, or as fast as demand allows. Choosing a mode that fits the goal is what keeps a guaranteed campaign on track from the first day to the last.

The available modes shape delivery in different ways. Even and smooth pacing spread the budget across the flight so the line item delivers a steady share each day, which suits a campaign whose only commitment is the total impression count by the end date. Front-loaded pacing delivers an accelerated share early and then evens out, which suits a campaign that needs presence at the start of a flight. ASAP pacing delivers as fast as inventory allows with no spread, which suits a short burst or a campaign that must clear quickly. A mode that does not fit the goal still delivers the volume the inventory allows, but it delivers it on the wrong shape, and on a guaranteed line item that shape is what determines whether the campaign lands smoothly or scrambles at the end.

The mismatch shows up as uneven delivery against a steady goal. A line item meant to spread evenly but set to front-loaded or ASAP can burn through most of its volume in the opening days, then sit short of inventory for the rest of the flight and finish under its number. A line item that needs an early start but is set to even pacing can deliver too little upfront to satisfy the advertiser's launch window. Either way the total may be technically reachable while the delivery curve fails the commitment.

A few signs point to a pacing mode that does not fit the goal:
  • A line item with a steady end-of-flight guarantee is set to front-loaded or ASAP pacing with no early-presence requirement to justify it
  • Delivery reporting shows a steep early curve followed by a flat or declining tail, or a slow start that never catches the daily share the goal needs
  • The pacing mode was left at a default rather than chosen against the campaign's actual delivery requirement
You may want to confirm that each guaranteed line item's pacing mode matches the shape its goal requires. One approach is to read the delivery curve for each line item against its flight and goal, then set the mode to even or smooth where the commitment is a steady total and reserve front-loaded or ASAP for campaigns with a stated reason to deliver early.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a streaming or broadcaster property, a sponsorship tied to a show's season or a tentpole live event often carries an early-presence requirement, so front-loaded pacing can fit it. A run-of-network guarantee across an on-demand catalog usually wants even pacing instead since its only commitment is the total by the end date. You may want to match the mode to whether the campaign has a real reason to deliver early or simply needs to land on its number smoothly.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content video property, a campaign timed to a sale window or a product launch may need front-loaded delivery to be present when shoppers arrive, while an always-on brand guarantee across category and how-to videos wants even pacing. You may want to confirm the mode reflects whether the campaign is tied to a dated commercial moment or is meant to spread steadily across the flight.

educate.learn_more_note: SpringServe's detailed configuration documentation for pacing modes and budget settings is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the pacing mode set on each guaranteed line item and compare it against the campaign's delivery goal and flight, then read the delivery curve to see whether the volume is arriving on the shape the goal requires.

springserve_ui.label: SpringServe console
steps: Open the demand or campaign section and list the active guaranteed line items with their flight dates and impression goals.
For each line item, open its budget or pacing settings and record the pacing mode (e.g., even, smooth, front-loaded, or ASAP).
Note the campaign's stated delivery requirement, whether it needs early presence, a dated burst, or a steady total by the end date.
Compare the mode against that requirement and flag any line item whose mode does not fit the shape its goal needs.

springserve_reporting.label: SpringServe reporting
steps: Build a delivery report broken out by line item and by day across the current flight, with impressions delivered against the daily and total goal.
Read the delivery curve for each line item to see whether it is spreading evenly, burning early, or starting slow.
Note any line item whose curve does not match its pacing mode's intent or the campaign's delivery requirement.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per guaranteed line item, the pacing mode, the campaign's delivery requirement, and the shape of the delivery curve across the flight. A line item whose mode fits its goal, even or smooth for a steady total and front-loaded or ASAP for a stated early-delivery reason, with a curve that matches, passes. A line item paced on a mode that does not fit its goal, such as front-loaded or ASAP on a steady end-of-flight guarantee, or a curve that diverges from the commitment, is a fail recorded with the line item and the mismatch.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the pacing mode, the delivery goal, and the delivery curve for each guaranteed line item above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that guaranteed line items at {{site_name}} are paced on a mode that does not fit their delivery goal, with steady-total guarantees set to front-loaded or ASAP pacing and delivery curves that burn early rather than spreading across the flight. The pacing mode in SpringServe sets the shape of delivery over time, not the total, so a mismatched mode can deliver the volume on the wrong curve and leave a guaranteed campaign short in the back half of its flight. {{voice_recommend}} reading each line item's delivery curve against its flight and goal, setting even or smooth pacing where the commitment is a steady total, and reserving front-loaded or ASAP for campaigns with a stated reason to deliver early. After the change, recheck the delivery curves to confirm each line item is pacing toward its goal on the shape the commitment requires.

audit_checks.title: Under-delivery risk is not monitored early enough to act on
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A guaranteed line item that falls behind its goal can still recover if the shortfall is caught with flight left to redirect inventory or adjust priority. Without monitoring on a cadence that surfaces the gap early, an under-delivering line item is often discovered at the end of the flight when the only remedy left is a make-good or a refunded guarantee, which costs revenue and the advertiser relationship.

educate.base:
A guaranteed line item in SpringServe carries a committed impression count, and the publisher is on the hook for delivering it. Whether that commitment is met depends not only on how the line item is paced but on whether anyone is watching its progress against the goal while the flight is still running. Under-delivery is recoverable when it is caught early because there is still flight left to raise priority, widen targeting, or shift inventory toward the lagging line item. Caught late, the same shortfall is a missed guarantee with no time left to fix it.

The gap between a recoverable shortfall and a missed one is monitoring cadence. A line item that is pacing behind on day three of a thirty-day flight has most of its flight left to make up the difference. The same line item discovered behind on day twenty-eight has almost none. The total it needs has not changed, but the runway to deliver it has nearly run out. Monitoring that surfaces the lag while the runway is long is what turns a delivery problem into a delivery adjustment rather than a make-good.

SpringServe reports delivery against goal for each line item, which makes the lag visible, but the report only helps if someone reads it on a regular cadence and acts on what it shows. The audit question is whether under-delivery risk is being tracked deliberately, through a scheduled report, an alert, or a recurring review, or whether shortfalls surface only when a campaign ends or an advertiser asks why the numbers came up short.

A few signs point to under-delivery risk that is not monitored in time:
  • No scheduled report, alert, or recurring review tracks delivery against goal for guaranteed line items during the flight
  • Shortfalls are discovered at or near the end of a flight rather than partway through, when little can be done
  • The publisher cannot say which guaranteed line items are currently pacing behind their goal without building a report from scratch
You may want to confirm that under-delivery risk is tracked on a cadence that leaves room to act. One approach is to set a scheduled delivery-against-goal report or an alert for guaranteed line items and review it on a fixed interval, so a lagging line item surfaces while there is still flight left to recover it.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a streaming or broadcaster property, guaranteed sponsorships tied to a season or an event have a hard end date, so a shortfall caught late cannot be recovered after the window closes. You may want to monitor delivery against goal frequently for time-bound sponsorships since their flights are often short and the room to recover a lag is narrow.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content video property, a guarantee timed to a sale or launch window ends when the commercial moment passes, so under-delivery has to be caught while the window is still open. You may want to review delivery against goal early and often for campaigns tied to a dated commercial event, where a make-good after the moment has little value to the advertiser.

educate.learn_more_note: SpringServe's detailed documentation for delivery-against-goal reporting and pacing alerts is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review whether a scheduled report, alert, or recurring review tracks delivery against goal for guaranteed line items during the flight, and check the current pacing status of active guarantees to see whether any are already behind.

springserve_ui.label: SpringServe console
steps: Open the demand or campaign section and list the active guaranteed line items with their flight dates, impression goals, and delivery to date.
For each line item, compare delivery to date against the share of the flight elapsed to see whether it is on pace, ahead, or behind.
Check whether any scheduled report or alert is configured to track delivery against goal for guaranteed line items.
Record which line items are currently pacing behind and whether any monitoring exists to surface that lag before the flight ends.

springserve_reporting.label: SpringServe reporting
steps: Build a delivery-against-goal report for active guaranteed line items, with impressions delivered, the goal, and the percentage of the flight elapsed.
Identify line items where delivery to date trails the share of flight elapsed by a margin that the remaining flight may not recover.
Note whether this report is run on a fixed cadence or only when a problem is suspected, given that a one-time read does not catch a lag that develops mid-flight.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether under-delivery risk is monitored on a regular cadence for guaranteed line items and notes which active line items are currently pacing behind their goal. A setup with a scheduled report, alert, or recurring review that surfaces lagging line items while flight remains passes. A setup with no monitoring, where shortfalls would only surface at the end of a flight, is a fail recorded along with any line items already pacing behind that the absent monitoring would have missed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether delivery against goal is monitored on a cadence and which guaranteed line items are currently pacing behind above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not monitor under-delivery risk on guaranteed line items early enough to act, with no scheduled report, alert, or recurring review tracking delivery against goal during the flight. Under-delivery is recoverable when it is caught with flight left to raise priority or shift inventory, but a shortfall found at the end of a flight leaves only a make-good or a refunded guarantee. {{voice_recommend}} setting a scheduled delivery-against-goal report or an alert for guaranteed line items in SpringServe and reviewing it on a fixed cadence, so a lagging line item surfaces while there is still time to recover it. After setting up the monitoring, confirm that any line item currently pacing behind is flagged and has a recovery plan before its flight ends.
